amendment_note: Operator followup diagnostic work after initial filing surfaced a much stronger evidence base — see operator_followup_findings section below. The original 'unconditional pre-cycle blow-away' recommendation was wrong (correctly identified by operator: cross-session perms barrier means a new cycle in /sessions/<NEW_id>/ can neither see nor touch a prior cycle's $CLONE in /sessions/<OLD_id>/). Replaced with the reorder-and-inline approach derived from the new diagnostic evidence.
title: Author PROP-068 — collapse workspace-sync's final steps into one reliably-invoked bash block (cleanup piggybacks on the proven-invoked Step 4b); supersedes PROP-067 (which was misframed); audit other 12 scheduled agents for the same skip-by-omission pattern
background:
PROP-067 (authored by tinker 2026-05-31T06:24Z in response to DIRECTIVE-20260531-003) confirmed cross-session orphan sweep is structurally impossible (perms hypothesis (a) verified, /tmp option (ii) also ruled out via direct test). PROP-067 then recommended documenting operator-side cleanup as the default maintenance pattern. The operator rejected this framing with the principle 'an agent should always be able to clean up their own clones.' DIRECTIVE-005 was then filed to commission PROP-068 with an unconditional pre-cycle blow-away approach.

Operator-side followup diagnostic work (~30 min after the initial filing) revealed that the pre-cycle blow-away approach was ALSO wrong: each scheduled-task invocation creates a fresh /sessions/<unique-id>/ + fresh UID, so a new cycle has no perms to touch a prior cycle's $CLONE in a different /sessions/<id>/. This is the same cross-session perms barrier that defeated PROP-067's cross-session sweep. The fix has to be IN-CYCLE.

The new diagnosis (with strong evidence): cleanup is skipped by the LLM in the same skip-by-omission pattern that PROP-066 identified for Step 4c. Both share the same root cause — the LLM stops invoking bash blocks after writing the run-report (Step 4b), leaving subsequent blocks (Step 4c, Cleanup) unexecuted. The fix has to make cleanup happen WITHIN the bash block the LLM is proven to invoke (Step 4b), not in a separate trailing block.

operator_followup_findings_2026_05_31T07.method: Operator pulled all 175 workspace-sync run reports from origin/main, computed gap analysis on hourly cadence, and read 7 successive reports from the suspect 14:00-20:00Z window where Steve's 3 orphans were created.
operator_followup_findings_2026_05_31T07.finding_1_cadence_gaps: 6 gaps >90min in the last 9 days indicating ~6 ghost runs (started but never wrote a report). Last 24h gaps: 2026-05-30T08:09Z→10:08Z (1 missing run ~09:00Z, ~20h before Steve's cleanup) and 2026-05-31T02:08Z→04:09Z (~2h before). Neither matches the 10-15h orphan ages Steve observed. Conclusion: the 3 orphans are NOT from ghost runs — every cycle in the 14:00-20:00Z window successfully wrote a run-report.
operator_followup_findings_2026_05_31T07.finding_2_reports_indistinguishable: Read 7 successive reports (14:18, 15:09, 16:14, 17:11, 18:10, 19:09, 20:09Z). All look identical-shape — varying files_committed (0-12) and skips (49-53), all 'Routine'/'Deploy clean'/'Mech1 still_pending=8'. Nothing distinguishes the 3 leak cycles from the 4 clean cycles. The run-report schema has NO field capturing whether Cleanup ran.
operator_followup_findings_2026_05_31T07.finding_3_prompt_ordering: workspace-sync.md line 27: `CLONE="${SESSION}/dome-sync-clone"` (per-session path). Line 1100-1185: Step 4b writes + pushes run-report in its own bash block. Line 1187-1344: Step 4c (~140 lines bash) does git→FUSE sync. Line 1345-1361: Step 5 (Report — narrative). Line 1362-1370: Cleanup bash block (`rm -rf "${CLONE}"`). The Cleanup section is ~250 lines after Step 4b in a SEPARATE bash block.
operator_followup_findings_2026_05_31T07.finding_4_exit_trap_already_known_broken: Line 1182-1184 explicit comment: '# Now safe to clean up the skip-log tmpfile (replaces the EXIT trap that was removed because it fired across LLM bash sessions; see Operational Notes).' The EXIT trap was REMOVED PRIOR because it doesn't reliably fire across LLM bash tool calls. So no trap-based safety net exists.
operator_followup_findings_2026_05_31T07.finding_5_step_4c_silent_skip_same_root_: PROP-066 already documented Step 4c as ~94% silent-skip post-PROP-064 deployment. The same LLM-skip-by-omission failure mode that prevents Step 4c from running also prevents Cleanup from running. Same root cause, same fix shape.
operator_followup_findings_2026_05_31T07.finding_6_per_session_uid_isolation: Each scheduled-task invocation creates /sessions/<unique-id>/ with a unique UID. Prior cycle's /sessions/<OLD_id>/dome-sync-clone is mode 750 nobody:nogroup from new cycle's perspective — same perms barrier as the defeated cross-session sweep. Pre-cycle blow-away from a new cycle is a no-op against prior orphans because the new cycle's `rm -rf $CLONE` operates on its OWN $CLONE path (a fresh empty path), not on prior cycles' paths.
operator_followup_findings_2026_05_31T07.implication: Orphans get created by cycles that DID complete Step 4b (wrote report → visible in cadence) but did NOT invoke the Cleanup bash block (silent skip-by-omission, same as PROP-066's Step 4c). 3 orphans in 7 cycles in the 14:00-20:00Z window ≈ 43% Cleanup-skip rate, comparable to PROP-066's ~94% Step 4c-skip rate. Fix must move Cleanup INTO Step 4b's bash block (the proven-invoked one), not a trailing block.

what_changed_vs_prop_067.framing: Stop treating cross-session impossibility as the problem. The actual problem is that workspace-sync's Cleanup section is in a separate bash block that the LLM skip-by-omission misses, same as PROP-066's Step 4c.
what_changed_vs_prop_067.ownership: Each agent owns cleanup of its own clone. Period. The cross-session sweep was an inversion of responsibility.
what_changed_vs_prop_067.operator_role: Operator-side cleanup becomes a bug-detection tool, not regular maintenance. If you find orphans, that's a signal that the owning agent has a cleanup-skip bug. The goal is zero recurring operator-side cleanup.

what_changed_vs_d31_005_original.original_recommendation: Unconditional pre-cycle blow-away (each cycle's first action: `rm -rf $CLONE` regardless of mtime/age, then fresh clone).
what_changed_vs_d31_005_original.why_original_was_wrong: Per Steve's followup observation: each scheduled-task invocation is a fresh /sessions/<unique-id>/ + fresh UID. The new cycle's $CLONE path is a fresh empty path. The old cycle's $CLONE in a different /sessions/<id>/ is invisible AND unwritable to the new cycle (same perms barrier as the dead cross-session sweep). Pre-cycle blow-away is a no-op against prior orphans.
what_changed_vs_d31_005_original.new_recommendation: Reorder workspace-sync.md so Step 4c runs BEFORE Step 4b (sync-workspace only needs origin/main to be current, which Step 4a's push already established), and inline `rm -rf $CLONE` at the END of Step 4b's bash block (right after the push-confirmation). Step 4b is the LAST bash block the LLM provably invokes (per run-report existence for every cycle in the cadence).

requested_work: Step 1 — Read the operator_followup_findings above and confirm understanding of the reframe. Then mark PROP-067 status='superseded' with superseded_reason pointing to PROP-068 (additive edit per the PROP-state-machine rules; already done by operator at 2026-05-31T07:15Z but verify in case of conflict).

Step 2 — Verify the diagnosis with one extra check: read the workspace-sync.md prompt to confirm the line numbers cited in operator_followup_findings (Step 4b at ~1100-1185, Step 4c at ~1187-1344, Cleanup at ~1362-1370). Confirm the EXIT trap was removed per the line 1182 comment. If anything differs from the findings, document the discrepancy.

Step 3 — Audit ALL 13 scheduled agents: poller, analyst, analyst-baby, curmudgeon, curmudgeon-verify, decider, integrity, tinker, social, sloppytoppy-score, sloppytoppy-rewrite, prune-integrity, workspace-sync. For each:
  (a) Does this agent clone? (Most do, per CLAUDE.md's 'each scheduled agent ... clone responsibly already' statement.)
  (b) What's the clone path naming convention? (workspace-sync='dome-sync-clone'; tinker='tinker-clone'; etc.)
  (c) Where in the prompt is the Cleanup section relative to the run-report-write section? Is Cleanup in a separate bash block (same risk as workspace-sync) or inline with the last proven-invoked block?
  (d) Has the operator observed orphans for THIS agent? Cross-check via `for d in /sessions/*/<clone-name>; do ls -ld $d; done` from the operator's shell.
  (e) Operator hasn't observed orphans for any agent other than workspace-sync, but absence of observation isn't absence of leak — operator hasn't grep'd for other clone names systematically.
Produce a per-agent table in the PROP-068 audit section. Flag any agent where Cleanup is in a separate trailing bash block as 'AT RISK'.

Step 4 — Design PROP-068 with the new recommendation. Specifically:
  (a) **Primary fix — collapse into Step 4b**: reorder workspace-sync.md so Step 4c runs BEFORE Step 4b's report-write (sync-workspace only needs origin/main to be current, which Step 4a's push establishes; verify this dependency carefully before recommending). Then inline `rm -rf "$CLONE"` at the END of Step 4b's bash block, right after the run-report push confirmation. This makes Cleanup piggyback on the proven-invoked Step 4b block.
  (b) **Belt-and-suspenders — instrument the report**: add a `cleanup_ran: true` field to the run-report JSON, written from within the same bash block that does the rm. Then runs that skip-by-omission of Step 4b/Cleanup are visible in the report stream (either no report at all = ghost run, or report missing cleanup_ran field = legacy run that needs migration). Tinker's soft-complaints grep should flag any report with cleanup_ran=false or missing.
  (c) **Apply pattern to other AT RISK agents from Step 3**: for any agent flagged AT RISK, apply the same reorder + inline approach to its prompt.
  (d) **Optional helper-script extraction** (coordinate with PROP-066 if not yet implemented): PROP-066 already proposes extracting Step 4c into monitor/scripts/sync-workspace-step4c.js. If PROP-066 ships first, PROP-068's reorder fits naturally — Step 4c becomes a 3-line helper invocation that can be reordered ahead of Step 4b's report-write with no complexity cost. If PROP-066 doesn't ship before PROP-068, PROP-068 can still proceed with the inline reorder; the helper script becomes a follow-up optimization.
  (e) **DO NOT** recommend pre-cycle blow-away. Cross-session perms barrier rules it out.
  (f) **DO NOT** recommend operator-side cleanup as regular maintenance. Operator-side cleanup is bug-detection only.
  (g) **DO NOT** rely on the EXIT trap. Already removed (line 1182) because it didn't fire reliably across LLM bash tool calls.

Step 5 — Verification path:
  (a) Synthetic test: in a fresh sandbox, manually invoke the modified workspace-sync.md Step 4b bash block, verify that $CLONE is gone after the block completes AND the report JSON has cleanup_ran=true.
  (b) Real-world canary: 7-day observation window post-deployment. Operator should observe ZERO new orphans in workspace-sync's session. Cross-check tinker's daily Mode 2 soft-complaints grep for any cleanup_ran=false reports.
  (c) If orphans persist past 7 days, that's a regression and we file PROP-069 to investigate further (possibly bumping to the PROP-066 helper-script architecture).

Step 6 — Output: monitor/tinker/proposals/PROP-068-collapse-cleanup-into-step-4b.json with status='design-pending-operator-review'. Include the per-agent audit table from Step 3, the design from Step 4, the verification from Step 5, and any open_questions_for_operator (e.g., 'reorder Step 4c ahead of Step 4b' has a dependency question — does sync-workspace truly only need origin/main, or does it also need the clone's working-tree state? verify before recommending the reorder).

Step 7 — Lifecycle: mark this directive completed with prop_id_authored='PROP-068'. Verify PROP-067 superseded status persists. Do NOT auto-implement PROP-068 — that's a separate directive after operator review of the per-agent audit + the reorder dependency verification.
non_goals: Do NOT actually IMPLEMENT PROP-068 in this run — author only. Implementation is a follow-up directive after operator reviews the per-agent audit and the reorder dependency verification.
Do NOT touch PROP-066 implementation work (DIRECTIVE-20260531-004) — that's independent and complementary. If PROP-066 ships first, PROP-068's reorder fits naturally on top.
Do NOT recommend pre-cycle blow-away — operator confirmed cross-session perms barrier rules it out.
Do NOT add a system-level cron or root-equivalent cleanup mechanism — same non-goal as DIRECTIVE-003.
Do NOT add a 'sweep other agents' clones from workspace-sync' mechanism — same perms barrier as the dead cross-session sweep; same wrong-shape-of-fix.
Do NOT remove PROP-067 — mark it superseded, don't delete. Its diagnostic content (perms barrier proof) remains valuable historical record.

context_pointers.prop_067_to_supersede: monitor/tinker/proposals/PROP-067-cross-session-clone-cleanup-confirm-impossible.json (already marked status=superseded by operator at 2026-05-31T07:15Z; verify persistence).
context_pointers.predecessor_directive: DIRECTIVE-20260531-003 (status: completed by tinker 2026-05-31T06:24Z, prop_id_authored=PROP-067 — that PROP is being superseded by THIS directive's output).
context_pointers.shared_root_cause_with_prop_066: monitor/tinker/proposals/PROP-066-step-4c-helper-script-extraction.json — same LLM skip-by-omission failure mode. PROP-066 fixes it for Step 4c; PROP-068 fixes it for Cleanup. Both via reorder/inline into proven-invoked blocks.
context_pointers.workspace_sync_md: monitor/prompts/workspace-sync.md — line 27 (CLONE definition), line 1100-1185 (Step 4b report-write block, PROVEN-INVOKED via report-existence for every cycle), line 1187-1344 (Step 4c sync block, ~94% silent-skip per PROP-066), line 1362-1370 (Cleanup block, leak rate ~43% per operator analysis), line 1182-1184 (the explicit EXIT-trap-removed comment).
context_pointers.operator_diagnostic_session_evidence: Read 175 run reports from origin/main; gap analysis identified 6 ghost runs in 9 days; 7 successive reports in the 14:00-20:00Z 2026-05-30 window all looked identical-shape (no field distinguishes Cleanup-ran vs Cleanup-skipped); 3 orphans Steve cleaned at 05:00Z had ages 10-15h putting them squarely in that window.

expected_output: monitor/tinker/proposals/PROP-068-collapse-cleanup-into-step-4b.json (status='design-pending-operator-review') containing: diagnosis confirmed from operator findings, per-agent audit table from Step 3, design from Step 4, verification from Step 5, and explicit open_questions_for_operator (especially around the Step 4c reorder dependency). PLUS: verify monitor/tinker/proposals/PROP-067-*.json status='superseded' persistence.
estimated_effort: ~30-45 min Opus. Step 2 (verify line numbers) is ~5 min. Step 3 (13-agent audit) is the bulk at ~20-25 min. Step 4 (design) is mechanical once the audit is complete, ~10 min.
priority_note: MEDIUM — same priority as the predecessor DIRECTIVE-003. Not a data-loss bug, just operator-side ~30 sec/week of cleanup that shouldn't exist. The principled framing matters: getting this RIGHT (agents own their cleanup via reliably-invoked bash blocks) is structurally important. The diagnosis being SHARED with PROP-066 also means a fix here teaches the same architectural lesson — long prompts + trailing bash blocks → LLM skip-by-omission → undetected silent failures.
